text: The surgeon docks the robotic system with a camera port, two working ports, and an assistant port after accessing the peritoneum. Using hook cautery, the gastrocolic ligament is incised and extended towards the hepatic flexure. The avascular plane is mobilized, allowing the colon to fall inferiorly aided by reverse Trendelenburg positioning. The duodenum is identified, and a Kocher maneuver is performed.

The surgical assistant retracts the duodenum medially and the transverse mesocolon inferiorly, exposing the dissection plane. The surgeon uses laparoscopic ultrasound to identify the ampulla's location, aided by preoperative pancreatic biliary stents for precise localization.
The surgeon performs a longitudinal duodenotomy, revealing the ampullary structures within the duodenum.

Stay sutures are placed in the duodenum to enhance exposure and retraction. The biliary stent is visible, and a suture is placed in the ampulla for retraction. Ultrasound is used to identify the size and margins of the ampullary mass. Cautery dissection is then employed to transect the mucosa circumferentially around the lesion.

The surgeon dissects down to the pancreas, identifying and transecting the pancreatic and bile ducts. A stay suture is placed in the pancreatic duct to prevent retraction into the pancreatic parenchyma, aiding in the identification of ductal anatomy. The remaining specimen is then transected and sent to pathology.

The surgeon sutures the common bile duct and pancreatic ducts together to form a common septum. Using 5-0 PDS sutures, the duodenal mucosa is approximated to the mucosa of the bile and pancreatic ducts in an interrupted circumferential manner. The biliary stent aids in retraction and ensures proper suture placement and patency during the reconstruction.

The surgeon observes the patent pancreatic and bile ducts with well-apposed duodenal mucosa to ductal mucosa. The duodenotomy is closed using a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a single-layer running technique, opting for a longitudinal closure to reduce tension on the suture line.
The surgeon inspects the suture line at the end of the ampullectomy procedure, ensuring proper closure. The abdominal cavity is irrigated, and no drain is placed.
